title: Verify Which Elements May Contain Which Children
summary: Build and verify semantic content models in invalid parent-child incident through prediction, parsed-structure inspection, explanation, cumulative code, and changed-case transfer.
objectives: Explain semantic content models from purpose and parsed structure.
Apply semantic content models while retaining prior HTML requirements.
Diagnose the named misconception in a changed content and interaction scenario.

starterFiles.html: <!-- Continue the Heat Relief Network artifact here. -->

steps.title: Predict the invalid parent-child incident relationship · theory
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 1: Which claim preserves semantic content models when the styling and content change? Scenario: invalid parent-child incident.
steps.why: Verify Which Elements May Contain Which Children keeps meaning testable instead of depending on appearance. This is checkpoint 1 in invalid parent-child incident.

content.title: invalid parent-child incident evidence
content.text: Choose the claim that preserves content meaning when visual styling and browser recovery are removed.

hints: Verify Which Elements May Contain Which Children checkpoint 1: name the content relationship before choosing markup.
Verify Which Elements May Contain Which Children checkpoint 1: compare source, parsed structure, and keyboard route.
Verify Which Elements May Contain Which Children checkpoint 1: choose the smallest native relationship that survives changed styling.

options.text: Invalid parent-child incident case 1: if invalid nesting produces the intended pixels, the parent-child relationship has no user or interoperability cost.
Invalid parent-child incident case 1: check the element content model, validate the source, and compare the parsed DOM before accepting the nesting.
Invalid parent-child incident case 1: indentation overrides the content model because it makes author intent obvious to the browser.

options.text: Invalid parent-child incident case 2: keep the defect because one browser recovered the invalid parent-child incident source.

Invalid parent-child incident case 2: a plausible render is recovery evidence, not proof. Check the element content model, validate the source, and compare the parsed DOM before accepting the nesting.
Invalid parent-child incident case 2: replace all semantic elements with div elements before locating the failed relationship.

steps.title: Read the invalid parent-child incident relationship · theory
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 5: Which claim preserves semantic content models when the styling and content change? Scenario: invalid parent-child incident.
steps.why: Verify Which Elements May Contain Which Children keeps meaning testable instead of depending on appearance. This is checkpoint 5 in invalid parent-child incident.

content.text: HTML content models define what kinds of children and descendants an element may contain. Browsers may move or close invalid nodes during parsing, so the DOM can differ from source. Conformance validation plus source-to-DOM comparison reveals broken list, paragraph, interactive, and phrasing-content relationships. Apply it to invalid parent-child incident.

hints: Verify Which Elements May Contain Which Children checkpoint 5: name the content relationship before choosing markup.
